periods.name: Colonial Brazil
periods.description: After Portugal claimed the territory in 1500, the region was gradually transformed from a trading outpost into a large plantation colony dependent on enslaved African labor. Over three centuries, settlement expanded far inland, the colonial economy grew around sugar and later gold, and periodic conflicts revealed the tensions of imperial rule.

events.description: In this first phase, the Portuguese limited their presence to coastal trading posts, exchanging goods for brazilwood with Indigenous peoples rather than establishing permanent settlements.
The Portuguese fleet commanded by Pedro Álvares Cabral landed on the Brazilian coast and claimed the territory for the Portuguese Crown, initiating first contact with Indigenous populations.
Portugal dispatched its first colonization expedition, led by Martim Afonso de Sousa, to establish permanent settlements and assert territorial control against French incursions.
Portugal divided the territory into fifteen hereditary captaincies granted to private nobles, who bore the costs of colonization in exchange for land rights and administrative authority.
Most captaincies had failed to sustain themselves, with only Pernambuco and São Vicente showing real viability, leading the Crown to reconsider the model.
Portugal replaced the captaincy system with a centralized royal government, founding Salvador as the colonial capital.
The first Jesuit missionaries arrived with the new government, going on to play a major role in Indigenous relations and education for the next two centuries.
The Portuguese and Spanish Crowns were united under Philip II of Spain, a period known as the Iberian Union, which exposed Brazil to Spain's enemies, particularly the Dutch.
Under Spanish rule, Portuguese settlers accelerated territorial expansion well beyond the borders set by the 1494 Treaty of Tordesillas.
Portugal restored its independence from Spain through the Portuguese Restoration War, ending sixty years of Iberian Union.
Dutch forces were definitively expelled from northeastern Brazil, restoring full Portuguese control over the sugar-producing region after decades of occupation.
The Treaty of Madrid replaced the old Tordesillas boundary with a principle of effective occupation, formally recognizing territory that settlers had claimed far beyond the original colonial limits.
The Marquis of Pombal expelled the Jesuit order from all Portuguese territories, removing the missionaries who had administered large portions of the Indigenous population.
The colonial capital moved from Salvador to Rio de Janeiro, reflecting the shift of economic and strategic weight southward following the gold boom in Minas Gerais.
The Inconfidência Mineira, a conspiracy in Minas Gerais led by local intellectuals inspired by the American and French revolutions, was the first serious independence plot in Brazil.
The conspiracy was suppressed and its main leader, Tiradentes, was executed.
Napoleon's invasion of Portugal forced the entire Portuguese royal court to flee to Brazil under British naval escort, making Rio de Janeiro the seat of the Portuguese Empire.
The Crown opened Brazilian ports to friendly nations, ending the trade monopoly that had restricted Brazilian commerce for three centuries.

periods.name: United Kingdom of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves
periods.description: With the Portuguese court still resident in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil was elevated from colony to an equal constituent kingdom within the Portuguese realm. When the court returned to Lisbon and Portugal moved to restore colonial restrictions, separation became increasingly likely.

leaders.name: John VI
leaders.title: King of Portugal, Brazil and the Algarves

events.description: Brazil was formally elevated to the status of a kingdom equal to Portugal, a change that made reverting to colonial subordination politically untenable for local elites.
A liberal revolution in Portugal demanded the king's return to Lisbon and sought to draft a constitution.
The same Portuguese revolution also aimed to re-subordinate Brazil as a colony, removing the institutions created since 1808.
John VI returned to Portugal under constitutional pressure, leaving his son Pedro as regent in Brazil.
The Portuguese legislature ordered Pedro to return to Europe and moved to dismantle Brazilian institutions, convincing Brazilian elites that independence was preferable to recolonization.

periods.name: Empire of Brazil
periods.description: Brazil declared independence and established a constitutional monarchy under the House of Braganza, an unusual outcome in the Americas. The empire lasted nearly seven decades, maintaining national unity while neighboring Spanish American nations fragmented, and ended with a military coup that established a republic.

events.description: Pedro I declared Brazil's independence from Portugal on September 7, an event remembered as the 'Cry of Ipiranga,' and was crowned emperor.
The new monarchy kept Brazil unified as a single state, in contrast to the fragmentation into multiple republics seen across former Spanish America.
Brazil adopted its first constitution, establishing a constitutional monarchy with a powerful emperor holding a fourth 'moderating power' above the executive, legislative, and judicial branches.
The Confederation of the Equator, a republican separatist revolt in the northeastern provinces, reflected regional dissatisfaction with centralized imperial rule and was suppressed within months.
After a costly and inconclusive war against Argentina over the Cisplatine province, Brazil recognized the independence of Uruguay as a neutral buffer state brokered by Britain.
Facing military mutinies, popular protests, and loss of elite support, Pedro I abdicated in favor of his five-year-old son and returned to Europe.
Without an adult emperor, Brazil was governed by elected regents while liberals and conservatives debated how much power should rest with the central government versus the provinces.
The Cabanagem revolt erupted in the Amazon region, led by impoverished mixed-race and Indigenous peoples against local elites; it became one of the bloodiest uprisings in Brazilian history.
The Ragamuffin War began in Rio Grande do Sul, where cattle ranchers and liberal republicans fought for greater autonomy and lower taxes on cattle products; the conflict lasted a decade.
The Balaiada revolt broke out in Maranhão, a social uprising of the poor, escaped enslaved people, and deserters against local oligarchies.
Parliament declared fourteen-year-old Pedro II of legal age to assume the throne, a political maneuver to stabilize the country and end the instability of the regency.
The Eusébio de Queirós Law effectively ended the transatlantic slave trade under British pressure, though slavery itself continued domestically for decades.
The Paraguayan War began after Paraguay attacked Brazil and Argentina. It became the largest conflict in South American history, lasting until 1870.
The war resulted in the deaths of a large proportion of Paraguay's population, while the Brazilian military emerged strengthened and politically assertive.
The Law of the Free Womb declared that children born to enslaved mothers would be free, marking the beginning of a gradual legal process of abolition.
Princess Imperial Isabel signed the Golden Law, immediately abolishing slavery without compensation to slaveholders.
The abolition law alienated powerful landowners from the monarchy, weakening the political base Pedro II needed to survive the following year's crisis.
A military coup led by Marshal Deodoro da Fonseca overthrew Pedro II and proclaimed a republic; the emperor chose not to resist and was exiled to Europe.

periods.name: First Brazilian Republic
periods.description: The republic inherited a federalist structure that concentrated power in the hands of regional agricultural elites, particularly those of São Paulo and Minas Gerais. The period was marked by political arrangements that excluded most of the population from meaningful participation, punctuated by military interventions and social conflicts.

events.description: The Republic was proclaimed on November 15 through a military coup, ending the monarchy without significant popular mobilization.
Brazil's first republican constitution was promulgated, establishing a federal system modeled partly on the United States, with significant autonomy granted to the states.
Deodoro da Fonseca resigned amid a political and constitutional crisis and was succeeded by his vice president, Floriano Peixoto.
A naval revolt and a concurrent Federalist Revolution in the south challenged Peixoto's government; both were suppressed at significant human cost, consolidating the military republic.

Civilian elites from São Paulo and Minas Gerais took control of the federal government through a system of political bargaining known as 'café com leite' politics, excluding workers, the poor, and the military from power.
The War of Canudos began when the federal army attacked a large religious community in the northeastern sertão led by the mystic Antônio Conselheiro.
After three military expeditions were repelled, a fourth destroyed the settlement and massacred its population, leaving around 25,000 dead.
The Vaccine Revolt erupted in Rio de Janeiro after the government made smallpox vaccination compulsory, turning popular resistance into days of street fighting.
The government suspended the vaccination decree to end the unrest.
The Revolt of the Lash was a mutiny by Black sailors of the Brazilian navy protesting the continued use of flogging as punishment, a practice associated with slavery.
The government agreed to end flogging but later punished many of the mutineers regardless.
After German submarines sank Brazilian merchant ships, Brazil declared war on Germany and the Central Powers, becoming the only South American country to participate militarily in World War I.
The Modern Art Week in São Paulo brought together writers, painters, and musicians who challenged European academic traditions to define a distinctly Brazilian cultural identity.
Junior military officers attempted a rebellion at the Copacabana Fort in Rio de Janeiro, one of a series of uprisings reflecting the armed forces' growing opposition to oligarchic rule.

The global Great Depression caused coffee prices to collapse, devastating Brazil's export-dependent economy and undermining the political position of the São Paulo elite controlling the federal government.
Washington Luís broke with convention by nominating another São Paulo politician as his successor rather than a candidate from Minas Gerais.
A military-backed revolution led by Getúlio Vargas overthrew the government in response, ending the Old Republic.

periods.name: Vargas Era
periods.description: Getúlio Vargas dominated Brazilian politics for fifteen years, first through a revolutionary provisional government, then through an elected presidency, and finally through a self-proclaimed dictatorship known as the Estado Novo. His government pursued rapid industrialization, created the framework of Brazilian labor law, and built a powerful centralized state, while suppressing political opposition.

events.description: São Paulo launched the Constitutionalist Revolution, an armed uprising demanding a return to constitutional government after two years of Vargas ruling by decree.
The rebellion was suppressed after three months of fighting, but Vargas subsequently allowed a new constitution to be drafted.
A new constitution was enacted, introducing women's suffrage and secret ballots for the first time in Brazil, while also granting Vargas a four-year presidential term.
Vargas canceled upcoming elections, dissolved Congress, and imposed the Estado Novo, an authoritarian regime inspired partly by European fascism.
A new constitution accompanying the Estado Novo concentrated all power in the executive and banned political parties.
After German submarines sank Brazilian ships, Brazil entered World War II on the Allied side.
Brazil deployed approximately 25,000 soldiers to fight in Italy, the only Latin American country to send combat troops to Europe.
The Consolidation of Labor Laws codified workers' rights including minimum wage, paid vacation, and regulated working hours, shaping Brazilian labor relations for decades.
Facing growing opposition from civilian politicians and the military, Vargas was deposed by a coup of senior generals and sent into retirement, opening the way for democratic elections.

periods.name: Second Brazilian Republic
periods.description: Brazil's second democratic period began with competitive multiparty elections and a new constitution. It brought major urban and industrial growth, the construction of a new capital, and ambitious development programs, but also growing political polarization between nationalist and conservative forces that ultimately ended with a military coup.

events.description: A new democratic constitution was promulgated, re-establishing direct elections, civil liberties, and a multiparty system after years of authoritarian rule.
The Brazilian Communist Party, which had won over 10% of votes in 1945, was banned under Cold War pressure, and its elected representatives were expelled from office.

Vargas created Petrobras as a state-owned oil company under the nationalist slogan 'O petróleo é nosso' (The oil is ours), part of a strategy of using natural resources to drive state-led industrialization.
Facing a severe political crisis, military pressure for his resignation, and an assassination attempt against a journalist linked to the opposition, Vargas took his own life.
He left a political letter blaming foreign and domestic financial interests for his downfall.
Kubitschek launched his 'Fifty Years in Five' program, a rapid industrialization plan that attracted foreign automobile manufacturers and expanded infrastructure.
The new federal capital Brasília was inaugurated in the Brazilian interior after just four years of construction, a nationally unifying project designed by Lúcio Costa and Oscar Niemeyer.
Quadros resigned after just seven months in office in a move that appeared intended to pressure Congress into granting him broader powers.
Congress instead accepted the resignation, triggering a constitutional crisis over whether his left-leaning vice president, João Goulart, could take office.
To overcome military resistance to Goulart taking office, Congress temporarily adopted a parliamentary system that reduced presidential powers.
A national referendum restored full presidential powers to Goulart, who accelerated his agenda of nationalist economic policies and social reforms, including land reform proposals.
A military coup, supported by conservative civilian groups and tacitly backed by the United States, ousted Goulart, who fled into exile.
The coup ended democratic rule and inaugurated over two decades of military government.

periods.name: Military Dictatorship
periods.description: Five successive military presidents governed Brazil for twenty-one years. The regime suppressed political opposition, censored the press, and used systematic torture against dissidents. It also oversaw rapid economic growth in the late 1960s and early 1970s followed by economic decline, before initiating a controlled transition back to civilian rule.

events.description: Institutional Act No. 1 allowed the regime to strip citizens' political rights by executive decree and purge the civil service, military, and judiciary of opponents.
Mass student protests and strikes swept Brazilian cities, part of a global wave of unrest.
The government responded with Institutional Act No. 5, which suspended Congress, authorized arbitrary arrest, and imposed censorship, marking the harshest phase of the dictatorship.
Brazil won the FIFA World Cup in Mexico for the third time, and the regime heavily promoted the victory to boost its popular image during the most repressive period of authoritarian rule.
The Brazilian economy grew at over 10% annually during what became known as the 'economic miracle,' though the gains were distributed unequally.
Geisel announced a policy of slow, gradual, and controlled political liberalization, a top-down process designed to keep the military in charge of the transition.
Journalist Vladimir Herzog was found dead in a military detention center in circumstances the regime claimed were suicide but were widely understood to be murder.
AI-5 was revoked and an amnesty law freed political prisoners and allowed exiles to return.
The same amnesty law effectively prevented prosecution of military personnel for human rights abuses, a controversial trade-off whose legal implications persist.
The Diretas Já ('Direct Elections Now') movement mobilized millions of Brazilians in the largest street protests in the country's history, demanding direct presidential elections.
The constitutional amendment for direct elections was rejected in Congress by a narrow margin after intense lobbying by the military government.
Opposition candidate Tancredo Neves was elected president by the Electoral College, marking the formal end of military rule.
Neves fell ill before his inauguration and died without taking office; his vice president, José Sarney, assumed the presidency.

periods.name: New Republic (Nova República)
periods.description: Brazil's ongoing democratic period began after the end of military rule. A new constitution enacted in 1988 established broad civil and social rights, and the country has since held regular competitive elections. The period has included economic stabilization, sustained poverty reduction, a major corruption crisis, and recurring political polarization.

events.description: The first civilian government in over two decades faced chronic hyperinflation alongside the task of writing a new constitutional order.
The Cruzado Plan attempted to eliminate hyperinflation through a freeze on prices and wages; initially popular, it collapsed within months as shortages emerged.

The Federal Constitution, known as the 'Citizen Constitution,' was promulgated after extensive debate, establishing direct elections, an independent judiciary, and social rights to health and education.
The new constitution also established protections for Indigenous territories and Afro-Brazilian communities.
Brazil held its first direct presidential election in twenty-nine years, won by Fernando Collor de Mello, a young conservative governor who campaigned against corruption and waste.
The Collor Plan froze private bank accounts above a set threshold in an attempt to remove money from circulation and halt hyperinflation, disrupting the savings of millions of Brazilians.
A congressional investigation revealed that Collor's campaign treasurer was operating a broad influence-peddling scheme.
Facing near-certain impeachment, Collor resigned; the Senate still voted to impeach him and suspend his political rights for eight years.
The Real Plan, developed by Finance Minister Fernando Henrique Cardoso, introduced a new currency and broke Brazil's cycle of hyperinflation through coordinated monetary and fiscal policy.
The government accelerated the privatization of large state companies in telecommunications and energy, part of a broader shift toward market-oriented economic management.
A currency crisis forced Brazil to abandon its fixed exchange rate and float the real, causing a sharp devaluation and requiring an IMF stabilization package.
Brazil recovered relatively quickly from the crisis, without the severe social collapse seen in neighboring Argentina.
A global commodity boom strengthened Brazil's export economy just as the new government turned to redistributive social policy.

The Bolsa Família conditional cash transfer program was created, providing monthly payments to low-income families contingent on school attendance and vaccination, becoming one of the largest anti-poverty programs in the world.
Vast oil reserves were discovered in deepwater pre-salt layers off the coast of Rio de Janeiro, transforming Brazil's long-term energy prospects.

Brazil's economy grew 7.5%, one of the highest rates in its history, as Lula left office with approval ratings above 80%, a combination of social programs, rising wages, and favorable commodity prices producing the largest reduction in poverty and inequality in the country's modern history.

Mass protests swept Brazilian cities, initially triggered by a bus fare increase in São Paulo, and quickly broadened into discontent over public services, corruption, and the costs of hosting the 2014 World Cup and 2016 Olympics.

The Lava Jato (Car Wash) investigation, launched by federal prosecutors in Paraná, uncovered a vast scheme in which construction companies paid bribes to Petrobras executives and politicians for inflated contracts.
The commodity boom that had sustained years of growth faded, and Brazil entered a severe economic recession that deepened the political crisis around Rousseff's government.

The Senate voted to remove Rousseff from office on charges that her government had manipulated budget accounts to conceal a fiscal deficit; Rousseff and her supporters maintained the charges were a pretext for a politically motivated removal.
Congress approved a sweeping labor reform that reduced protections established since the 1943 CLT, which Temer presented as necessary to restore fiscal balance.
Congress also approved a constitutional amendment capping public spending growth for twenty years.
Deforestation in the Amazon increased sharply as the new government rolled back environmental oversight agencies and enforcement, drawing international criticism.
The COVID-19 pandemic killed over 700,000 Brazilians, the second-highest absolute death toll in the world, amid a public health response widely criticized for delays in vaccine procurement.
Lula defeated Bolsonaro in the presidential election's second round by a margin of less than two percentage points, the closest result in the history of the republic.

Thousands of Bolsonaro supporters stormed and ransacked the Presidential Palace, Congress, and the Supreme Court on January 8, days after Lula's inauguration, in an attempt to destabilize the incoming government.
Lula returned to the presidency for a third non-consecutive term, focused on rebuilding environmental policy and reactivating social programs.
Brazil rejoined international climate commitments and reinstated environmental agencies dismantled under the previous government, pledging to eliminate illegal deforestation in the Amazon by 2030.
Brazil hosted the G20 summit in Rio de Janeiro, using the presidency of the group to advance proposals for a global minimum tax on billionaires and increased debt relief for developing countries.
